quote: I'm gonna put a a new roof on it, and then I wanna put solar on it.
stewart_read: Steven names a planned roof replacement and solar install in the same breath. This is the exact trigger condition in cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned. Holland never bridges to Ion's in-house roofing capability at any point. The reactive read is that Holland simply didn't recognize the signal. There is no evidence of a strategic deferral — she never returns to roofing.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have intended to surface roofing on the callback call once the purchase closed. No evidence supports this; she makes no note of it and the roofing signal is never acknowledged.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1 and walk Holland through the schema bridge template. The customer handed her a two-revenue-stream opening — roof sale now, solar sale after close — and she walked past both. Ask her: 'What did you hear when he said roof and solar in the same sentence?' If she didn't hear the roofing signal, that's the training gap.

quote: So because I'm in San Diego right now.
stewart_read: Steven's current San Diego residence is an available proxy for bill pain, utility company, and usage patterns. San Diego Gas & Electric is among the highest-rate utilities in the country — a customer currently paying SDG&E rates is a strong bill-pain candidate. Holland could have run partial verify against the San Diego home ('What are you paying on your current bill out there?') to establish a qualification baseline and emotional anchor before the new home closes. She does not attempt it.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that bill data from a San Diego home is irrelevant to a Little River solar install. Partially valid — usage patterns transfer even if the utility does not. The coaching point is that the emotional anchor (bill pain) is portable even when the address is not.
coaching_implication: Show Holland that verify.average_bill and verify.interest_in_solar can be run against the customer's current home when the target home has no bill history yet. The bill pain is the person's pain, not the address's pain. This is a concrete opening she had and didn't use.

quote: Yeah. So I was actually gonna say so I think it would be, better if we scheduled, let's see.
stewart_read: Holland's only scheduling move produces a callback promise, not a locked appointment. No date, time, or duration is confirmed for the actual sit. No button-up elements are executed: no one-hour block stated, no phone-appointment specification, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. The outcome is structurally a soft callback, not a sit.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that booking a sit before Steven has keys is premature and that the callback is the correct intermediate step. This is partially defensible — but it does not explain why she ran zero verify items or captured no qualification data on this call.
coaching_implication: The callback structure itself may be correct given the timeline. The coaching gap is that Holland treated the callback as the finish line rather than as a bridge. She should have run as much of the verify section as possible on this call so the callback is a confirmation, not a cold restart.

quote: Well, thanks, Holland. I appreciate you calling.
stewart_read: By end of call, Holland has captured: customer name, general location (Little River), and a travel window. She has not captured: homeowner confirmation on the new property, anyone else on the title, interest-in-solar depth, utility company, average bill, roof type/age, or prior designs. That is zero of eight verify items against a required threshold of six of eight per schema verify.required_completion_threshold. This is a call-wide structural omission, not a single-moment failure.
alternative_hypothesis: Some verify items (roof age, prior designs) are genuinely not answerable before the home closes. This is true but accounts for at most two of eight items. The remaining six were available and not pursued.
coaching_implication: Use this call to show Holland what a verify-complete callback setup looks like versus what she delivered. The question for the 1-on-1: 'What information did you have at the end of this call that you didn't have at the start?' The answer is almost nothing — and that's the gap.

quote: I just kinda wanted to talk to somebody that was in that area just to see what, you know, maybe setting up a time after I purchase it.
stewart_read: Steven is not a passive inbound lead — he is actively planning a solar install as part of a home purchase sequence and reached out to find the right contact. This is a high-intent signal that maps directly to verify.interest_in_solar. Holland acknowledges the timeline but never asks 'what got you interested in solar?' or 'what are you hoping to get out of it?' — the emotional anchor question the schema identifies as the most important verify item.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have read Steven's planning posture as sufficient qualification and skipped the depth question to avoid redundancy. But the schema is explicit: depth matters more than the answer. A 60-second exploration here would have built the emotional anchor for the closer.
coaching_implication: Show Holland the schema note on verify.interest_in_solar: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Steven was ready to talk. Holland didn't ask.

quote: And then, yeah, I think that would be a little bit better just since you will get the keys hopefully by then. And I'll be real it'll be for real.
stewart_read: Holland's closing rationale — 'it'll be for real' — acknowledges the contingency risk (closing might not happen) but does not resolve it. The callback on the fourth is itself contingent on the April 24 closing proceeding. Holland has no locked sit, no verify data, and no protection against a no-show if the closing slips. Per setter_compensation_model.paid_on: sits, this call produces zero compensation-eligible output.
alternative_hypothesis: The contingency is real and Holland is correct to name it. The coaching gap is not that she named the contingency — it is that she did nothing to reduce the fragility of the outcome (no verify data captured, no partial qualification baseline established).
coaching_implication: Walk Holland through what a 'fragile callback' looks like versus a 'qualified callback.' A qualified callback has verify data, a named decision-maker, a bill anchor, and a specific time locked. This callback has none of those. If the closing slips, Holland restarts from zero.
